gh-w6-f1.answer:
Lambda expressions — concise anonymous functions for functional interfaces (single abstract method).

Requirements:
1. Define or use built-in functional interfaces: Predicate<String>, Function<Integer, Integer>, Consumer<String>, Supplier<Double>.
2. Implement each with a lambda — e.g., s -> s.length() > 5, n -> n * n, System.out::println, () -> Math.random().
3. Pass lambdas to methods: filter a list with Predicate, transform with Function.
4. Sort a list of strings by length using lambda: (a, b) -> Integer.compare(a.length(), b.length()).
5. Show method reference: list.forEach(System.out::println) and String::compareToIgnoreCase.

Key concepts:
• Syntax: (params) -> expression  OR  (params) -> { statements; return x; }
• Lambdas replace verbose anonymous inner classes.
• Target type: compiler infers from context (e.g., Comparator<String>).
• Variables used inside must be effectively final.
gh-w6-f1.realWorld:
Why it matters: Java 8+ code in Spring Boot, Hibernate Criteria, and Stream API is lambda-heavy. Event handlers, validators, and async callbacks all use functional interfaces. Interviewers expect fluent lambda + Stream code.

Compile & run:
javac LambdaExamples.java
java LambdaExamples

Push to java-week-6-java8:
git add LambdaExamples.java
git commit -m "Add LambdaExamples.java — functional interfaces and lambdas"
git push

Tip: If you see "variable used in lambda should be final" — assign to a final local copy before the lambda.

gh-w6-f2.answer:
Streams API — declarative pipeline for processing collections without explicit loops.

Requirements:
1. Create streams from List: list.stream() and Stream.of(1,2,3,4,5).
2. Intermediate ops: filter (evens), map (square), distinct, sorted, limit(5).
3. Terminal ops: collect(toList()), count(), max(), min(), reduce(0, Integer::sum).
4. Process List<Employee>: filter salary > 50000, map to names, collect.
5. anyMatch, allMatch, noneMatch on a stream of numbers.
6. Print each pipeline stage result so you understand lazy evaluation.

Key concepts:
• Stream does not store data — it pipes elements through operations.
• Intermediate ops return Stream (lazy); terminal ops trigger execution.
• Once consumed, a stream cannot be reused — create a new one.
• Prefer streams for filter/map/collect chains; simple loops are still fine for trivial cases.
gh-w6-f2.realWorld:
Why it matters: Spring Data returns streams; microservices aggregate data with Stream pipelines; reporting dashboards filter millions of rows. Streams + lambdas are the modern Java idiom — legacy for-loop chains look dated in code review.

Compile & run:
javac StreamExamples.java
java StreamExamples

Push:
git add StreamExamples.java
git commit -m "Add StreamExamples.java — filter, map, collect pipeline"
git push

Watch out: list.stream().map(...).collect() — forgetting collect() means nothing runs for some terminals, but map alone won't print — you need forEach or collect.

gh-w6-f3.answer:
Optional — a container that may or may not hold a value, designed to reduce NullPointerException.

Requirements:
1. Create Optional with Optional.of("value"), Optional.ofNullable(maybeNull), Optional.empty().
2. Check: isPresent(), isEmpty() (Java 11+).
3. Consume safely: ifPresent(v -> System.out.println(v)), orElse("default"), orElseGet(() -> computeDefault()).
4. Transform: map, flatMap — chain without null checks.
5. orElseThrow() when absence is an error.
6. Simulate findUserById — return Optional<User> instead of null.

Key concepts:
• Never use Optional.of(null) — throws NPE. Use ofNullable for possibly null values.
• Don't use Optional for fields or method parameters everywhere — mainly for return types.
• orElse vs orElseGet: orElseGet is lazy (supplier runs only if empty).
• Optional makes "missing value" explicit in the type system.
gh-w6-f3.realWorld:
Why it matters: Spring Data JPA methods return Optional<T>; REST APIs use Optional for 404 vs 200; config lookups avoid null. Code reviewers flag methods that return null without documentation.

Compile & run:
javac OptionalExamples.java
java OptionalExamples

Push:
git add OptionalExamples.java
git commit -m "Add OptionalExamples.java — safe null handling"
git push

Anti-pattern: if (opt != null) — Optional itself shouldn't be null. Methods should return Optional.empty(), not null.

gh-w6-f4.answer:
Java 8 Date/Time API (java.time) — immutable, thread-safe replacement for legacy Date and Calendar.

Requirements:
1. LocalDate: today(), of(2025, 6, 15), plusDays(7), isBefore/isAfter.
2. LocalTime: now(), of(14, 30), plusHours(2).
3. LocalDateTime: combine date and time, format with DateTimeFormatter.ofPattern("dd-MM-yyyy HH:mm").
4. Period.between(date1, date2) for days/months/years between dates.
5. Duration.between(time1, time2) for hours/minutes/seconds between times.
6. Parse a string to LocalDate using formatter.

Key concepts:
• All java.time types are immutable — plusDays returns new object.
• No timezone in LocalDate/Time — use ZonedDateTime for global apps.
• DateTimeFormatter for parse/format — thread-safe unlike SimpleDateFormat.
• Period for dates, Duration for times — don't mix them.
gh-w6-f4.realWorld:
Why it matters: Invoice due dates, subscription renewals, appointment booking, and audit timestamps all need correct date math. Bugs from mutable Calendar caused production outages — java.time is the standard in modern Java and Spring Boot.

Compile & run:
javac DateTimeExamples.java
java DateTimeExamples

Push:
git add DateTimeExamples.java
git commit -m "Add DateTimeExamples.java — LocalDate, Period, Duration"
git push

Common mistake: LocalDate.parse("15/06/2025") fails — default format is ISO yyyy-MM-dd. Use a formatter for dd/MM/yyyy.

gh-w6-f5.answer:
Multithreading — run multiple threads of execution concurrently within one JVM process.

Requirements:
1. Extend Thread: class MyThread extends Thread { public void run() { ... } } — start with start(), not run().
2. Implement Runnable: new Thread(new MyRunnable()).start() — preferred (composition over inheritance).
3. Create 2–3 threads printing their name and a counter (1–5).
4. Demonstrate Thread.sleep(500) — handle InterruptedException.
5. Use join() on a thread so main waits for worker to finish before printing "Done".
6. Optional: show race condition — two threads incrementing shared int without sync (wrong count).

Key concepts:
• start() creates new call stack; run() is just a normal method call on current thread.
• Main thread and worker threads run concurrently (on multi-core) or time-sliced.
• Shared mutable state without synchronization → race condition.
• synchronized keyword or locks fix critical sections — covered in TicketBookingSystem.
gh-w6-f5.realWorld:
Why it matters: Web servers handle thousands of concurrent requests on thread pools; Android runs UI on main thread and work on background threads; batch jobs process files in parallel. Understanding threads is prerequisite for Spring @Async and reactive programming.

Compile & run:
javac ThreadExamples.java
java ThreadExamples

Output order may vary between runs — that's concurrency.

Push:
git add ThreadExamples.java
git commit -m "Add ThreadExamples.java — Thread, Runnable, sleep, join"
git push

gh-w6-f6.answer:
Executor Framework — manage thread pools instead of manually creating Thread objects.

Requirements:
1. ExecutorService pool = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(3).
2. Submit Runnable tasks with execute() or submit() — at least 5 tasks.
3. Use Callable<String> that returns a result; Future<String> f = pool.submit(callable); String result = f.get().
4. ScheduledExecutorService: schedule a task after 2 seconds, scheduleAtFixedRate every 1 second (run 3 times then shutdown).
5. Always shutdown pool: pool.shutdown() and awaitTermination(10, TimeUnit.SECONDS).
6. Print thread name inside tasks to see pool reuse.

Key concepts:
• Thread pool reuses threads — cheaper than new Thread per task.
• Fixed pool: bounded concurrency; cached pool: grows/shrinks; single: one worker.
• Callable = Runnable with return value; Future = async result handle.
• get() blocks until task completes — use timeout in production.
gh-w6-f6.realWorld:
Why it matters: Tomcat, Spring Boot embedded server, and @Async all use thread pools under the hood. Misconfigured pools cause thread exhaustion or CPU thrashing — ExecutorService is how production Java scales concurrent work.

Compile & run:
javac ExecutorExamples.java
java ExecutorExamples

Push:
git add ExecutorExamples.java
git commit -m "Add ExecutorExamples.java — thread pool and Callable"
git push

Important: Failing to shutdown() leaks threads — JVM may not exit in IDE until pool is shut down.

gh-w6-f7.answer:
Employee Analytics — process employee data with Streams, Lambda, and Optional. Week 6 analytics project.

Requirements:
1. Employee record/class: id, name, department, salary, joinDate (LocalDate).
2. Seed List<Employee> with at least 8 employees across 3 departments.
3. Analytics (all via Streams):
   • Average salary by department (groupingBy + averagingDouble).
   • Top 3 highest paid employees.
   • Count employees per department.
   • Filter Engineering dept with salary > 70000.
4. findEmployeeByName(String): return Optional<Employee>.
5. Print formatted report to console.

Key concepts:
• Collectors.groupingBy, averagingDouble, counting.
• Comparator.comparingDouble for top-N: sorted(...).limit(3).
• Combine Stream pipeline readability with meaningful method names.
• Optional for lookup that may fail.
gh-w6-f7.realWorld:
Why it matters: HR dashboards, payroll reports, and BI queries are exactly filter/group/aggregate operations. Before SQL aggregation you often process in-memory collections — same Stream patterns as Java backend analytics microservices.

Compile & run:
javac EmployeeAnalytics.java
java EmployeeAnalytics

Push:
git add EmployeeAnalytics.java
git commit -m "Add EmployeeAnalytics.java — Stream analytics project"
git push

gh-w6-f8.answer:
Ticket Booking System — concurrent seat booking with thread safety. Demonstrates synchronization in a real scenario.

Requirements:
1. TicketCounter with fixed availableSeats (e.g., 10).
2. bookSeat(String userName) — decrement seats if available, else print "Sold out".
3. Multiple threads (10–20) call bookSeat concurrently simulating users.
4. Use synchronized on bookSeat OR ReentrantLock to prevent double-booking (race condition).
5. Print each successful booking with remaining seats; final count must be correct.
6. Use ExecutorService to launch concurrent booking threads.

Key concepts:
• Race condition: check-then-act (if seats > 0 then seats--) must be atomic.
• synchronized method/block: only one thread at a time in critical section.
• Without sync, two threads may both see seats=1 and both book — overbooking.
• Prefer higher-level concurrency utilities in production; synchronized teaches the core idea.
gh-w6-f8.realWorld:
Why it matters: IRCTC, BookMyShow, and airline reservation systems face exactly this problem — limited inventory, concurrent users. Database transactions and optimistic locking extend the same principle at scale.

Compile & run:
javac TicketBookingSystem.java
java TicketBookingSystem

Run multiple times — with proper sync, total bookings never exceed available seats.

Push:
git add TicketBookingSystem.java
git commit -m "Add TicketBookingSystem.java — synchronized concurrent booking"
git push

gh-w6-f9.answer:
Task Scheduler — background job execution using ScheduledExecutorService. Week 6 executor project.

Requirements:
1. Task interface or Runnable with task name and simulated work (Thread.sleep 1s).
2. ScheduledExecutorService scheduler = Executors.newScheduledThreadPool(2).
3. schedule(task, delay, TimeUnit) — run once after 3 seconds.
4. scheduleAtFixedRate(task, initialDelay, period, TimeUnit) — run every 2 seconds, 4 times.
5. Track task status: PENDING, RUNNING, COMPLETED — print status changes.
6. shutdown() after all tasks; awaitTermination with timeout.
7. Optional: submit immediate tasks via execute() alongside scheduled ones.

Key concepts:
• Fixed-rate: tries to run every N seconds regardless of task duration.
• Fixed-delay: waits N seconds after previous run finishes.
• Separate scheduler threads from worker logic for clarity.
• Always plan shutdown — scheduled pools keep JVM alive.
gh-w6-f9.realWorld:
Why it matters: Cron jobs, report generation, cache refresh, and heartbeat pings use schedulers. Spring @Scheduled wraps the same concept; Quartz and Kubernetes CronJob scale it for production.

Compile & run:
javac TaskScheduler.java
java TaskScheduler

Observe timed output in console.

Push:
git add TaskScheduler.java
git commit -m "Add TaskScheduler.java — ScheduledExecutorService project"
git push

gh-w6-f10.code:
# java-week-6-java8

Week 6 — Java 8 features, functional programming, and multithreading for Prathyu Academy.

## Topics covered
- Lambda expressions and functional interfaces
- Streams API (filter, map, collect, groupingBy)
- Optional for null-safe code
- java.time (LocalDate, Period, Duration)
- Thread, Runnable, sleep, join
- ExecutorService and ScheduledExecutorService
- Projects: Employee Analytics, Ticket Booking, Task Scheduler

## How to run
Requires JDK 17+.

```bash
javac *.java
java LambdaExamples
java StreamExamples
java TicketBookingSystem
```

## Files
| File | Description |
|------|-------------|
| LambdaExamples.java | Predicate, Function, method refs |
| StreamExamples.java | Stream pipeline demo |
| OptionalExamples.java | ofNullable, map, orElse |
| DateTimeExamples.java | LocalDate, Period, formatting |
| ThreadExamples.java | Thread, Runnable, join |
| ExecutorExamples.java | Fixed pool, Callable, Future |
| EmployeeAnalytics.java | Stream analytics project |
| TicketBookingSystem.java | Synchronized booking |
| TaskScheduler.java | ScheduledExecutorService |

## Java 8 quick reference
```java
list.stream().filter(x -> x > 0).map(x -> x * 2).collect(toList());
Optional.ofNullable(value).map(String::toUpperCase).orElse("N/A");
```

## What I learned
I learned to replace loops with Stream pipelines for readable data processing, use Optional instead of null checks, and understand why shared variables in threads need synchronization.
